frameworks.name: serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: Recent perceptual history biases current perceptual decisions.

core_question: Which aspects of prior perceptual decisions (sensory features, choices, or motor actions) drive serial dependence?
key_assumptions: Serial dependence reflects a genuine perceptual/decisional bias rather than response bias alone
Low-level sensory information, choices, and motor actions can be experimentally dissociated
manipulations: Motor action mapping across prior and test (same vs different keys)
Low-level stimulus similarity (color match/mismatch) across discriminations
Relevance of prior discriminations to the test feature
key_findings: Location discriminations were biased toward prior choices even when reported with different motor keys
Biased priors on an irrelevant feature (color) did not substantially shift subsequent location decisions
When color was the discriminated feature, biased prior locations no longer influenced location decisions
Shared low-level stimulus and motor features did not trigger serial dependence alone but boosted it
interpretations: Relevance across perceptual decisions is the key driver of serial dependence
Choice-level signals, not sensory or motor traces by themselves, carry the bias
